topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The polished mahogany of Dr. Silas Thorne’s office gleamed under the soft, recessed lighting. I stood just inside the doorway, my knuckles white where I gripped the strap of my expensive leather bag. He looked… different. The sneer that had been the centerpiece of my early existence was replaced by a calm, almost unnerving empathy. He adjusted his spectacles, a gesture so delicate it felt like a performance. “Come in, Elara,” he said, his voice a low, even baritone that used to send a jolt of pure panic through my chest. I walked to the chair opposite him, deliberately slow, letting the silence stretch until it became thick enough to choke on. I watched his hands as he rested them on the desk—hands that used to grip my backpack straps, hands that had once shoved me against the lockers. A slow, cold burn started low in my stomach, a familiar, unwelcome heat. I needed to see the cracks in this façade, the moment the monster slipped back into place.

[story 2]

I waited until he was alone, ostensibly reviewing files, but I knew he was waiting for me. My jaw was tight, my posture rigid, every muscle coiled with a tension that bordered on pain. When he finally looked up, his eyes—those sharp, assessing eyes—met mine without flinching. There was a flicker there, a ghost of the old antagonism, quickly smothered by something softer, something professional. "You look agitated," he observed, leaning back slightly in his leather chair. I didn't respond immediately. Instead, I let my gaze sweep over his tailored suit, cataloging every detail, searching for the imperfection that proved he was just a man, not a savior. A sharp, involuntary twitch pulled at the corner of my mouth. I wanted to shatter the quiet, to force him to acknowledge the residue of every petty slight, every moment of humiliation he had inflicted. I finally spoke, my voice pitched low, laced with a deliberate, cutting sarcasm that I hoped would sting him back.

[story 3]

The clinic smelled of aged paper and expensive sandalwood, a stark contrast to the stale gym socks and cheap cafeteria food of my youth. I entered, deliberately brushing against the edge of a bookshelf, a small, deliberate act of disrespect. He was seated by the window, sketching something in a large, leather-bound notebook. When he noticed me, he didn't jump or react with anger; he simply raised an eyebrow, a subtle acknowledgment that felt like a personal slight. A surge of intense frustration flooded me. I walked toward him, my steps purposeful, the sound of my heels clicking sharply on the hardwood floor. I stopped just short of his desk, leaning in so close that I could smell the faint, clean scent of his cologne. "You think you can just erase it?" I challenged, my voice trembling slightly, but not from fear—from a deep, simmering resentment. I watched his composure falter for just a second, a barely perceptible tightening around his eyes, and I knew I had struck a nerve.
